questions.question_text: All real estate advertising in Colorado must include:
questions.correct_answer: The employing broker's name or trade name
questions.explanation: Colorado requires all advertising to include the employing broker's name or licensed trade name. Blind ads without broker identification are prohibited.

questions.question_text: A broker posts a property listing on social media without any brokerage identification. This is:
distractors: Permitted if the post is unpaid
Allowed if the property address is included
questions.explanation: Internet and social media advertising must comply with the same rules as other advertising — the employing broker's name must be included. Blind ads are prohibited on all platforms.

questions.question_text: A team wants to use a team name that sounds like a real estate company. This is:
questions.correct_answer: Prohibited if it misleads the public
distractors: Permitted if the team has 5+ members
questions.explanation: Team names can be used but must not mislead the public about the nature of the entity. A name that makes a team appear to be an independent company would be misleading.

questions.question_text: A broker places a 'Sold' rider on a listing that belongs to another broker. This practice is called:
questions.explanation: Sign crossing — placing a sold sign on another broker's listing without permission — is prohibited in Colorado.

questions.question_text: Which statement about Colorado real estate advertising is correct?
questions.correct_answer: Online ads follow the same rules as print
distractors: Online ads are exempt from broker ID rules
Print ads need broker name but online do not
Only paid advertisements need broker name
questions.explanation: All advertising — print, online, social media — must comply with the same identification requirements. There is no exemption for digital platforms.

questions.question_text: In Colorado, real estate commissions are:
distractors: Determined by the employing broker only
questions.explanation: Commission rates are freely negotiable — there are no fixed or standard rates. Any suggestion otherwise violates antitrust principles.

questions.question_text: A broker offers a buyer a rebate at closing. Under Colorado law, this is:
questions.explanation: Rebates to principals in a transaction are permitted in Colorado as long as they are disclosed. There is no restriction on who may receive a rebate.

questions.question_text: A broker pays a referral fee to an unlicensed person who referred a buyer. This is:
questions.explanation: Referral fees may only be paid to licensed brokers or exempt parties. Paying referral fees to unlicensed individuals is a license law violation regardless of amount.

questions.question_text: A title company offers a broker a gift card for each closing referred to them. Under RESPA, this is:
questions.explanation: Inducements from settlement service providers that are tied to referrals violate RESPA. Gift cards per closing constitute illegal kickbacks.

questions.question_text: A seller tells a broker that another brokerage charges a lower commission rate. The broker should:
distractors: Report the competitor to the Commission
State that rates are set by the industry
questions.explanation: Commission rates are freely negotiable. The broker should discuss their value proposition and negotiate rather than claiming rates are fixed or reporting the competitor.

questions.question_text: A licensed broker wants to purchase a property they have listed for sale. The broker must:
questions.correct_answer: Disclose their licensed status and interest
distractors: Transfer the listing to another broker first
Get Commission pre-approval for the purchase
questions.explanation: Licensees must disclose their licensed status and any personal interest in a transaction. They can purchase their own listing but must make full disclosure.

questions.question_text: A broker's spouse is the buyer in a transaction the broker is handling. The broker must disclose:
questions.correct_answer: The relationship as a conflict of interest
distractors: Nothing if the spouse uses a different name
Only if asked by the other party
Only if the purchase price is below market
questions.explanation: Any relationship with parties that could constitute a conflict must be disclosed. A spousal relationship is a clear conflict of interest requiring disclosure regardless of circumstances.

questions.question_text: A broker sells their personal residence. They must disclose to the buyer that they are:
distractors: An owner — no additional disclosure needed
A broker only if they list it on MLS
questions.explanation: Licensees must disclose their licensed status when buying or selling their own property. This applies regardless of whether they use the MLS or list it privately.

questions.question_text: Failure to disclose a conflict of interest in a transaction can result in:
distractors: A written warning with no consequences
No penalty if the deal closes smoothly
questions.explanation: Failure to disclose conflicts of interest is grounds for disciplinary action, which can include fines, suspension, or revocation of the license.

questions.question_text: A broker has a business partnership with the home inspector they recommend. This relationship is:
distractors: Normal business practice needing no disclosure
questions.explanation: Any relationship that could constitute a conflict of interest must be disclosed. A business partnership with a recommended service provider is a clear conflict.

questions.question_text: A Colorado broker can fill in blanks on which type of documents?
distractors: Any contract the parties agree to
questions.explanation: Licensees may fill in blanks on Commission-approved forms but may not draft legal documents. Using custom or attorney-drafted forms for fill-in purposes is not authorized.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks a broker to draft a custom contract provision not found on any standard form. The broker should:
questions.correct_answer: Recommend the buyer consult an attorney
distractors: Add it as an informal handwritten note
Include it in the additional provisions section
questions.explanation: Brokers may not draft legal documents. Custom provisions should be prepared by an attorney. The broker can fill in blanks on approved forms but not create new legal language.

questions.question_text: After all parties sign a contract, the broker must provide copies to:
questions.explanation: The employing broker must ensure copies of all executed documents are provided to all parties — not just the broker's own client.

questions.question_text: A seller wants to carry financing for the buyer. The broker must ensure that:
distractors: The seller obtains a lending license
The interest rate matches market rates
questions.explanation: Seller financing requires specific Commission-approved forms and disclosures covering interest rate, payment schedule, balloon payments, and due-on-sale provisions.

questions.question_text: A broker drafts a purchase agreement from scratch for a complex commercial deal. This activity is:
distractors: Permitted if the broker has 10+ years experience
questions.explanation: Drafting legal documents from scratch constitutes the unauthorized practice of law, regardless of the transaction type or the broker's experience level.
